coredata.dc:description: Heavy metal contamination, particularly from Pb(II), poses severe environmental and health risks due to its toxicity and persistence in ecosystems. This study presents an innovative and sustainable adsorbent composed of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs), derived from sodium alginate and immobilized on a sodium alginate hydrogel (HL-NCDs), for the efficient extraction of Pb(II) from water. The experimental results indicate that PbN and PbC interactions, particularly involving the NH moieties within the carbon quantum dots, are the predominant mechanisms facilitating Pb(II) removal, as confirmed by density functional theory (DFT) calculations. These interactions are substantiated by thermodynamic analyses and charge distribution studies, which highlight the enhanced affinity of HL-NCDs towards Pb(II) ions. The novel HL-NCDs composite material not only demonstrates excellent Pb(II) adsorption efficiency but also suggests broad applicability in water treatment technologies, marking a significant advancement in the use of biomass-derived materials for environmental remediation.

head.ce:title: Immobilization nitrogen-doped sodium alginate-derived carbon quantum dots on sodium alginate hydrogel for enhanced Pb(II) removal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metal contamination, particularly from Pb(II), poses severe environmental and health risks due to its toxicity and persistence in ecosystems. This study presents an innovative and sustainable adsorbent composed of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs), derived from sodium alginate and immobilized on a sodium alginate hydrogel (HL-NCDs), for the efficient extraction of Pb(II) from water. The experimental results indicate that PbN and PbC interactions, particularly involving the NH moieties within the carbon quantum dots, are the predominant mechanisms facilitating Pb(II) removal, as confirmed by density functional theory (DFT) calculations. These interactions are substantiated by thermodynamic analyses and charge distribution studies, which highlight the enhanced affinity of HL-NCDs towards Pb(II) ions. The novel HL-NCDs composite material not only demonstrates excellent Pb(II) adsorption efficiency but also suggests broad applicability in water treatment technologies, marking a significant advancement in the use of biomass-derived materials for environmental remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal contamination in water sources constitutes a significant global environmental and public health issue [1,2]. Among these contaminants, Pb(II) is designated a priority pollutant by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) due to its ability to biomagnify through the food chain and its severe health implications. Exposure to Pb(II) can result in neurological damage and developmental delays in children [3,4]. Developing effective and sustainable technologies for the remediation of lead-contaminated water is therefore of critically important. Compared to other techniques such as chemical precipitation [5], ion exchange [6], and membrane separation [7], adsorption is often preferred for its simplicity and low toxicity [8]. Consequently, synthesizing of an efficient adsorbent for Pb(II) remains a crucial focus for researchers.

Hydrogel materials, known for their high surface area and numerous active sites, have been extensively investigated for their potential in water purification [9–11]. These hydrogels are rich in carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, enabling them to chelate with divalent or multivalent metal ions and form insoluble gels [12–14]. Sodium alginate (SA), a natural and renewable polysaccharide polymer sourced from brown seaweed, is extensively utilized as a precursor for hydrogel fabrication because of its unique properties. For instance, Li et al. [15] documented a cost-effective hydrogel made from soybean dregs and poly (acrylic acid) that exhibited substantial adsorption capacities for Zn(II), Fe(III), Cu(II), and Cr(III), recorded at 121.2, 78.5, 75.4, and 41.7 mg g−1, respectively. Chen et al. [16] developed an environmentally friendly, physically crosslinked double-network hydrogel utilizing chitosan, sodium alginate, and calcium ions, employing an innovative approach that combines sol-gel transition and internal gelation techniques. Despite their potential, hydrogels often exhibit lower adsorption capacities, emphasizing the need for innovative enhancements.

Carbon dots (CDs), a cutting-edge zero-dimensional nanostructured carbon material, are characterized by impressive chemical stability, low toxicity, robust mechanical strength, outstanding photoluminescence, and high solubility [17,18]. CDs are reported to detect different heavy metals such as Hg, Zn, Cd, Cu, Fe, and Pb, and their study is focused on the sensing properties [19]. These sensing probes are now applied in monitoring mode but does not focus on heavy metal capture or water filtration [20,21]. Their functional groups (-OH, -COOH, -NH2) offer selective detection and adsorption capabilities for metal ions [22,23]. Recently, various biomass materials have been used as carbon precursors to create CDs more economically and efficiently [24,25], but their small size (<10 nm) complicates recovery, limiting their practical applications in adsorption. One strategy to address this limitation is loading or encapsulating CDs onto a support to enhance their adsorption performance.

Doping CDs with elements such as nitrogen (N), boron (B), and sulfur (S) significantly enhance the density of functional groups on their surfaces, thereby improving their sensitivity and adsorption capacity for metal ions [26,27]. Liao et al. prepared N-CDs using ethylenediamine as a nitrogen source via the hydrothermal method, demonstrating excellent dispersibility and stability, and exhibiting good selectivity towards p-nitrophenol [28]. Wang et al. used various nitrogen sources to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) and found that, compared to three other nitrogen sources, N-CDs prepared with ethylenediamine not only exhibited the highest fluorescence intensity but also the maximum quantum yield, reaching 51.39 % [29]. In this study, we utilize SA not only as a precursor for synthesizing CDs but also as a base material for forming hydrogels, thereby offering dual functionality. Additionally, building on previous studies, we introduce ethylenediamine as a nitrogen source to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs). This research aims to explore the synergistic effects of NCDs compounded with SA hydrogel in the adsorption of lead ions, proposing a novel, efficient, and environmentally sustainable approach for water purification. This not only addresses the urgent need to remove lead from drinking water but also contributes to the broader environmental goal of developing sustainable remediation technologies.

Sodium alginate (C6H7NaO6, 99 %), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, 99.5 %), and calcium chloride (CaCl2, 97 %) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). No further purification was performed on these analytical reagents before use.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using a K-Alpha instrument from Thermo Scientific (ESCALAB™250Xi, USA) to analyze the elemental and chemical states of the samples. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed using IRTracer 100 instrument from Shimadzu (Japan). The surface morphology and microstructures of the as-prepared materials were characterized using field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-6480 A, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI TECNAI G2, USA). The zeta potential of the as-prepared materials was determined using a Zeta potentiometer (Zeta PALS Brookhaven). The concentration of Pb(II) remaining in solution was determined by an Inductive Coupled Plasma Emission Spectrometer (ICP, ICPE-9800, Shimadzu, Japan). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was measured using a Particle Size and zeta potential Analyzer (Malvern, Britain).

NCDs were synthesized using a well-established hydrothermal method as described in previous literature [30]. Initially, 2 g of SA powder were thoroughly mixed with C2H8N2 and 100 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a 200 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 10 h. After the treatment, the resulting product was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was then transferred into a dialysis bag for separation and purification. Throughout the dialysis process, ultra-pure water was refreshed every 10 h for approximately two days, ultimately resulting in the production of the final forms of the materials. The designations NCDs-, NCDs, and NCDs+ refer to the NCDs synthesized using increasing volumes of C2H8N2, specifically 120 μL, 240 μL, and 480 μL, respectively.

The HL-NCDs were prepared as follows. Firstly, a mixture was prepared by combining 0.4 g of SA with varying quantities of NCDs (4, 10, 20, 100, and 200 mg) in 20 mL of deionized water, which was then vigorously stirred magnetically for 4 h to ensure uniformity. Subsequently, this mixture was slowly dripped into 60 mL 0.1 mol·L−1 CaCl2. The resulting NCDs hydrogels were immersed in the CaCl2 solution for 12 h to achieve complete gelation. The final product, labeled as HL-xNCDs, where x indicates the concentration levels, such as 1, 2, and 3, was vacuum sealed for preservation.

Batch adsorption experiments for Pb(II) were conducted using the synthesized HL-xNCDs as the adsorbent. Each experiment was replicated three times to ensure reliability, and the adsorption capacities (mg·g−1) were calculated using Eq. 1 [31].

In this equation, q e (mg·g−1) represents the adsorption capacity at equilibrium, V (L) is the volume of the solution, C 1 (mg·L−1) is the initial concentration of Pb(II), C 2 (mg·L−1) is the final concentration of Pb(II), and m (g) refers to the mass of the dry HL-NCDs.

In the kinetic study, HL-NCDs were administered at a concentration of 0.25 ± 0.01 g·L−1, with an initial Pb(II) concentration set at 100 mg·L−1. The equilibrium adsorption capacity (qe) for Pb(II) was recorded at multiple time points: 0, 30, 60, 120, 240, 480, 720, 960, 1200, 1440, 1800, and 2160 min. To analyze the data, various kinetic models were applied, including pseudo-first-order (Eq. 2), pseudo-second-order kinetics (Eq. 3), Elovich kinetics (Eq. 4), and W&M kinetics (Eq. 5) [32].

First-principles calculations employing density functional theory (DFT) were conducted using the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional, which falls under the Generalized Gradient Approximation (GGA) category [34]. These calculations were executed with the Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP) code [35,36]. The electron wavefunctions were modeled using a plane-wave basis set, with an energy cutoff set at 500 eV. Convergence thresholds for electronic and ionic relaxations were established at 10−6 eV and 0.01 eV/Å, respectively. Additionally, the DFT-D3 method was employed to include van der Waals interactions [37–39].

The coronene (C24H12) was used to model the structure of CDs. Two types of NCDs structures, NCDs-A and NCDs-B, were constructed by doping N atoms into the internal and edge C atom of CDs, respectively. The adsorption energies of the Pb atom on CDs were calculated using the following equation (Eq. 10) [40].

are the total energies of the CDs, Pb atom, and the total adsorption system, respectively.

In this study, a simple, eco-friendly method was employed to create a high-efficiency adsorbent for the removal of Pb(II). As illustrated in Fig. 1a, SA was utilized both as the bio-sources precursor for CDs and as the natural scaffold for the construction of 3D hydrogels. During the hydrothermal process, ethylenediamine monohydrate was introduced as the nitrogen source to obtain NCDs. The SA hydrogel, rich in surface-active groups, facilitates cross-linking with NCDs without chemical additives and offers additional adsorption sites for heavy metal ion aggregation. Consequently, the resulting HL-NCDs adsorbent are characterized by rich functional groups, excellent adsorption capabilities, and strong fluorescence properties.

To demonstrate the successful doping of the nitrogen source, the elemental composition of the NCDs was analyzed as shown in Table.S1. As the dosage of ethylenediamine increased, the proportion of nitrogen also rose accordingly (0.52–1.15 %). The FTIR spectra (Fig. 1b) reveal a broad band around 3500–3200 cm−1 corresponding to the OH stretching vibration modes. A weak peak at approximately 2931 cm−1 is attributed to the CH anti-symmetrical stretching vibration [41]. The C=O/COOH absorption peak at 1720 cm−1 relates to the tensile vibration of carboxylic acid, and a peak at about 1387 cm−1 corresponds to the -C=O stretching vibration [42]. The enhanced absorption peak at 2931 cm−1 in NCDs, due to the presence of NH-O type hydrogen bonds, increases with ethylenediamine concentration, along with the peak at 1549 cm−1 attributed to the N-H/C-N tensile vibration. Overall, the functional groups COOH, NH-O type hydrogen bonds, and NH in NCDs have increased compared to CDs and are positively correlated with the dose of ethylenediamine. Excessive ethylenediamine, however, may reduce the -COOH functional group. FTIR spectroscopy confirms that the surface of NCDs is rich in oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups (-OH, NH, -COOH), which enhances the binding of heavy metal ions.

To understand the changes in the optical properties of CDs and NCDs during their interaction with the adsorbent, photoluminescence spectra were analyzed. Remarkably, ethylenediamine significantly enhanced the fluorescence intensity of the material. As depicted in Fig. 1c, NCDs exhibit characteristic emission peaks that indicate enhanced fluorescence properties at an optimum emission wavelength of 446 nm, unchanged from CDs but with significantly improved fluorescence due to the targeted regulation of the CDs structure by ethylenediamine. Additionally, the amino group in ethylenediamine increases water solubility, thereby improving the quantum yield, fluorescence intensity, and chemical stability of the CDs.

SEM and TEM images were utilized to analyze the morphology and structure of the NCDs. Figs. 1d-e demonstrates that the NCDs exhibit a spherical and uniform shape, with diameters typically between 4 and 7 nm. This uniformity suggests a well-regulated synthesis process, marked by notable CDs agglomeration. This agglomeration suggests effective nucleation and growth phases during synthesis. Compared to more varied CDs, the NCDs present a larger average diameter, attributed to the presence of ethylenediamine. The amino groups in ethylenediamine are pivotal, altering the binding dynamics between the carbon dots and Ca2+, thereby enhancing the synthesis efficiency of the CDs. High-resolution TEM image, as shown in the inset of Fig. 1f further reveal the crystalline structure of the NCDs. Lattice fringes measuring 0.24 nm correspond to the graphene (100) crystal planes [43], highlighting the structural integrity and precise crystallinity of these engineered nanoparticles. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of NCDs (Fig. S7) shows a diffraction peak at 2θ = 23°, corresponding to a graphite structure, which further confirms the results from HR-TEM.

Fig. 1g illustrates the XPS full spectrum of NCDs, displaying distinctive peaks for N1s, Na1s, C1s, and O1s. Gaussian curve fitting was employed to analyze the high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s, C1s, and O1s to obtain their chemical states in detail. As shown in Fig. 1h, the N1s XPS spectrum is characterized by two peaks, indexed as -N-H- (401.7 eV) and -C-N (399.8 eV). Similarly, the O1s spectrum, displayed in Fig. 1i, includes three peaks identified as -C-O- (531.4 eV), -C=O (535.9 eV), and H-O-C (533.0 eV). Additionally, the C1s spectrum, presented in Fig. 1j, is composed of three peaks centered at 284.9, 286.3, and 288.4 eV, corresponding to C-C/C-C/CHx, CO, and -COOR, respectively.

These results indicate that the synthesized NCDs are rich in oxygen-containing and nitrogenous functional groups, which are beneficial for the binding of heavy metal ions. The prominent presence of CN groups not only underscores the stability of the carbon dot structure but also highlights the functionality of the amino groups. These groups possess lone electron pairs capable of coordinating with heavy metal ions to form stable complexes, thereby enhancing the adsorption capabilities of the materials. This observation aligns with the conclusions drawn from FTIR analysis, further affirming the potential of these modified NCDs in heavy metal ion adsorption applications.

The initial pH of the solution significantly influences the adsorption process due to its impact on interactions between the surface species of the adsorbent and the adsorbate ions, as well as the surface charge characteristics. The zeta potential curve recorded over the pH range of 1.0 to 7.0 indicates that the point of zero charge (pHZPC) for NCDs is 2.4 (Fig. 2a). Below this pHZPC, the surface of the NCDs becomes positively charged, causing mutual repulsion with cationic heavy metals [44]. Consequently, this repulsive effect restricts the diffusion of Pb(II) to the NCDs surface, which is unfavorable for Pb(II) adsorption by the adsorbent. Conversely, NCDs exhibit a negative surface charge when the pH exceeds 2.4 [45]. As the positive charge on the NCDs surface decreases, this repulsive effect gradually weakens or disappears, thereby reducing resistance to Pb(II) adsorption and increasing the adsorption capacity. The negative charge on the NCDs surface provides ample adsorption sites, creating electrostatic attraction for Pb(II), enabling significant adsorption on the NCDs surface. Increasing the negative charge on the sorbent surface did not significantly alter the removal of Pb(II), indicating that electrostatic interaction is not a major factor affecting the adsorption reaction.

Fig. 2b illustrates the removal efficiency of Pb(II) by HL-5NCDs across various pH levels. Specifically, the adsorbent is positively charged below a pH of 2.4, and the competitive effect of protons diminishes as pH increases. The optimal removal efficiency occurs at pH 6, where the adsorbent carries a negative charge, achieving an adsorption capacity of 271.15 mg·L−1. The decrease in Pb(II) adsorption efficiency at pH 7.0 results from the complexation reaction between Pb(II) ions and hydroxide ions (-OH) at higher pH levels. Given that the pH of the Pb(II) solution during adsorption experiments was maintained between 5.0 and 6.0, pH 6 was selected for optimal adsorption performance.

Additionally, the effects of different NCD concentrations on the HL-NCDs composite materials were examined under identical conditions. As shown in Fig. 2c, incorporating NCDs into the hydrogel matrix significantly enhances its ability to adsorb Pb(II). An increase in the amount of NCDs is associated with an enhanced adsorption capacity of the HL-NCDs composite. However, an excessive amount of NCDs will lead to the inevitable aggregation, potentially diminishing the overall adsorption capacity. Consequently, HL-5NCDs was selected as the focus for subsequent experiments.

Wastewater typically contains multiple coexisting ions that could potentially compete with Pb(II) for adsorption sites, thus impacting the efficiency of Pb(II) removal. Therefore, common cations were employed as competing ions to evaluate their competitive adsorption with Pb(II) on HL-NCDs. According to Fig. 2d, in solutions with low concentrations of monovalent cations (Na+ and K+), HL-NCDs' ability to adsorb Pb(II) was substantially unchanged. Conversely, in solutions containing divalent cations (Ca2+ and Mg2+), a slight decrease in adsorption capacity was observed. These cations' competitive adsorption order with Pb(II) was found to be Ca2+ > Mg2+ > Al3+ > Na+ > K+. These disparities can be explained by differences in charge densities and hydration radii.

To investigate the adsorption kinetics of Pb(II) on HL-5NCDs, several kinetic models were applied, including pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order kinetics, elovich kinetics and W&M kinetics (Eq. 5). The calculated model parameters are summarized in Table 1 . Notably, the pseudo-second-order kinetics model provided the best fit for the data (R2 = 0.9835) as shown in Fig. 2f-h, suggesting that the adsorption of Pb(II) on HL-5NCDs is predominantly chemisorptive, possibly complemented by some degree of physical adsorption [46]. The efficient removal of Pb(II) by the HL-5NCDs composite is attributed to the abundant nitrogen and oxygen groups on the surface of NCDs and the surface-active groups on the SA hydrogel. These features provide numerous adsorption sites, enhancing the adsorption capacity of the composite. Furthermore, the Elovich model also closely fits the adsorption data (R2 = 0.9821), underscoring the complex nature of the adsorption process. The adsorption kinetics can be divided into three distinct stages as per the intraparticle diffusion model (Fig. 2e): an initial rapid adsorption phase (t < 240 min), a slower adsorption phase (240 min < t < 1440 min), and a plateau phase where adsorption reaches equilibrium (t > 1440 min). The initial rapid adsorption is attributed to the abundance of available adsorption sites and a significant potential difference between the adsorbent and the Pb(II) solution, facilitating the rapid diffusion of Pb(II) ions to the HL-5NCDs [47]. This stage is characterized primarily by surface diffusion. In the subsequent phase, as the concentration gradient decreases and some adsorption sites become occupied, the adsorption rate slows down, with both internal diffusion and surface adsorption playing dominant roles [8]. The adsorption capacity stabilizes after 1440 min, indicating that a dynamic equilibrium has been reached. This stabilization suggests that the overall adsorption process is influenced by both intraparticle diffusion and external mass transfer, highlighting the complex dynamics involved in reaching adsorption equilibrium [48].

The sorption behavior of HL-5NCDs exhibits a discernible pattern, characterized by a rapid initial increase followed by a gradual rise until reaching saturation. At lower concentrations, a heightened concentration gradient between the solid and liquid phases occurs initially due to the presence of Pb(II), which enhances mutual attraction between Pb(II) and HL-5NCDs, resulting in higher adsorption levels. However, as the active sites become predominantly occupied by Pb(II), the adsorption capacity plateaus, and remaining constant as the concentration approaches saturation. Adsorption isotherms are essential for analyzing adsorption behavior and assessing the adsorption capacity of an adsorbent. In this study, the experimental data were modeled using four isotherm models (Langmuir, Freundlich, Temkin and Sips). The results, along with the corresponding diagrams, are presented in Figs. 2i, j, and k, and summarized in Table 2 . The findings confirm that the adsorption of Pb(II) by HL-5NCDs predominantly follows the Langmuir model, suggesting that adsorption occurs through a homogeneous monolayer process. Additionally, the R2 values of the Sips model are 0.9787, 0.9907, and 0.9979, indicating that the Sips model is also suitable for describing the adsorption process of Pb(II) on HL-5NCDs. This suggests that the distribution of active sites on HL-5NCDs is uneven at the boundaries, resulting in an heterogeneous adsorption process.

The equilibrium constant kL represents the strength of the adsorption process, with a larger kL indicating a stronger adsorption capacity. At 298 K, the kL value for the Langmuir model is 0.2306, which is higher than the values of 0.1858 and 0.0839 at 308 K and 318 K, respectively. This indicates that there is a decrease in adsorption capacity with an increase in temperature, suggesting that higher temperatures inhibit the adsorption process [49]. The maximum theoretical adsorption capacity, calculated using the Langmuir isotherm at 298 K, was found to be 346.81 mg·g−1. In addition, the reusability of HL-5NCDs was also investigated (as shown in Fig. S6), and results show that HL-5NCDs presents relatively good reusability with Pb(II) elimination of 92.5 % and 73.8 % in the first and fifth runs, further indicating the potential application of HL-5NCDs in Pb(II) elimination.

The viability and characteristics of the Pb(II) removal process were assessed through thermodynamic parameters, revealing a temperature-dependent nature of Pb(II) adsorption. The thermodynamic parameters, including the change in Gibbs free energy (ΔG°, kJ·mol−1), change in enthalpy (ΔH°, kJ·mol−1), and change in entropy (ΔS°, kJ·mol−1), were evaluated as follows:

Here T (K) represents the Kelvin temperature, R (8.314 J·mol·K−1) denotes the gas constant, and Ka (L·mol−1) signifies the adsorption equilibrium constant. The corresponding parameters are presented in Table 4. The ΔG0 values were calculated as −2.6489, −2.8733, and − 3.0618 kJ·mol−1 at 298 K, 308 K, and 318 K, respectively. Evidently, the ΔG0 value decreased with increasing temperature, indicating that the adsorption mechanism favored lower temperatures. The binding of Pb(II) to the surface of HL-NCDs reduces directional disorder, resulting in a negative enthalpy value, ΔH0 (−3.5089 kJ·mol−1), suggesting that Pb(II) adsorption on the surface of HL-NCDs is an exothermic process. Moreover, the negative ΔS0 value (−20.6827 J·mol−1·K−1) signifies a reduction in disorder at the solid-liquid interface during the adsorption process.

The fluorescence responses of NCDs to varying concentrations of Pb2+ are depicted in Fig. S1, where the maximum emission wavelength is observed at 436 nm. Notably, the fluorescence intensity of the NCDs decreases progressively with increasing concentrations of Pb(II). This pattern suggests that NCDs experience varying degrees of fluorescence quenching in the presence of different concentrations of Pb(II), indicating the potential of this material for specific detection of Pb(II) in future applications.

In our previous studies within our group, the maximum adsorption capacity of HL-CDs was found to be 305.21 mg·L−1, whereas nitrogen-doping significantly enhanced the adsorption performance by 13.63 %. Previous studies have shown that while carbon materials possess good performance, they can be enhanced by incorporating heteroatoms such as nitrogen. Specifically, nitrogen functional groups can assist in anchoring cations, thereby improving adsorption performance [50]. Moreover, Sun et al. demonstrated that nitrogen doping in the right concentration range (4–8 wt%) could improve carbon electrical conductivity and provide faster charge-transfer kinetics [51]. To explore the mechanisms of nitrogen functional groups and changes in charge transfer capabilities, methods such as XPS and DFT calculations were applied to demonstrate the activating effect of nitrogen doping on HL-CDs.

FTIR and XPS characterizations of the adsorbent post-adsorption were instrumental in elucidating the mechanism of Pb(II) adsorption by HL-5NCDs. As illustrated in Fig. 3a, the FTIR spectra of HL-5NCDs post-adsorption reveal significant alterations in key spectral peaks, which highlight the interaction dynamics during the adsorption process. The changes are evident at 3500–3200 cm−1 (indicative of -OH groups), 2978 cm−1 (NH stretching), 1065 cm−1 (R-O-C=O), 1612 cm−1 (-COOH), and 815 cm−1 (CO stretching). These modifications confirm the involvement of oxygen-containing and amino groups in the adsorption reaction, with notable variations in -COOH, NH, and CO peaks indicating their roles as primary reaction sites for Pb(II) adsorption. The modifications in the -OH and -COOH peaks suggest their potential involvement in reducing Pb(II) through complexation reactions [52]. XPS analysis further supported these findings. The full survey spectra (Fig. 3b) retained the characteristic peaks of nitrogen and oxygen, with two new photoelectron peaks appearing at 139.2 and 144.0 eV for Pb 4f, post-reaction, confirming the attachment of Pb(II) to the surface of HL-5NCDs. The N1s peak showed substantial variation after adsorption (Fig. 3c), highlighting the critical role of the nitrogen functional group in the adsorption process. Specifically, the -N-H- group's lone pair electrons interact with Pb(II) to form a stable complex, enhancing the material's adsorption capacity. The O1s spectra (Fig. 3d) showed an increase in the peak area of metal-O groups from 14.66 % to 17.58 % following the adsorption of Pb(II), indicative of PbO bond formation. Additionally, Fig. 3e displays the characteristic Pb 4f spectrum of HL-5NCDs post-adsorption, featuring a low energy band at 139.3 eV (Pb 4f7/2) and a high energy band at 144.0 eV (Pb 4f5/2), due to spin-orbit splitting. These bands are consistent with the characteristics of PbO and Pb(OH)2 [53]. These results from both FTIR and XPS analyses revealed a significant reduction in the peaks associated with -N-H-, -O-C=O, and –C-N- in HL-5NCDs post-adsorption. These findings support the hypothesis that complexation reactions between oxygen-containing functional groups and metal ions play a pivotal role in the adsorption of Pb(II) onto HL-5NCDs, thereby facilitating effective adsorption and precipitation.

The SEM image of HL-5NCDs post-adsorption revealed significant changes in the surface morphology, substantiating the adsorption of Pb(II) onto the surface of HL-5NCDs. Further analyses using SEM-EDS and STEM-EDS elemental mapping analyses highlighted a distribution of lead that closely aligns with the distribution of nitrogen and oxygen. This correlation confirms that N and O groups are the primary adsorption sites, as depicted in Fig. 3f and g. Notably, the uniform adsorption of Pb2+ across HL-5NCDs post-adsorption indicates effective interaction. Furthermore, the energy spectrum of Ca2+ became noticeably darker after adsorption, suggesting a cation exchange mechanism between Ca2+ and Pb2+.

DFT calculation was employed to analyze the mechanism of adsorption enhancement of NCDs and to substantiate experimental findings with theoretical insights. The model for NCDs was based on the structure of coronene (C24H12), with two variants, NCDs-A and NCDs-B, constructed by doping nitrogen atoms into internal and edge carbon atoms, respectively. Fig. 4a displays the optimized structures where Pb atoms predominantly adsorb at the edges. In the CDs and NCDs-A structures, Pb atoms align at a significant angle relative to the carbon plane, forming PbC bonds with edge carbon atoms. Conversely, in the NCDs-B structure, the Pb atoms lie nearly planar with the carbon point due to the proximity of nitrogen atoms, favoring the formation of PbN bonds. The bond lengths in CDs and NCDs-A for PbC were found to be 2.84 Å, whereas a shorter PbN bond length of 2.44 Å in NCDs-B suggests a stronger PbN interaction. The binding energies calculated for these structures, as shown in Fig. 4c, are negative, indicating that NCDs can capture Pb atoms energetically favorably. Particularly, NCDs-B, with nitrogen doping at the edge, exhibited a more negative binding energy (−0.93 eV) compared to CDs, aligning with the shorter bond length. Furthermore, Fig. 4b illustrates the charge-density differences post-adsorption, with yellow and blue regions indicating electron accumulation and depletion, respectively. This distribution highlights charge interactions between Pb(II) and different structural planes, suggesting chemical bond formation. Bader charge analysis revealed that Pb donates 0.22, 0.16, and 0.06 electrons to the surface in various configurations, corroborating the significant chemisorptive capacity of NCDs towards Pb(II). These findings affirm the theoretical predictions and provide a deeper insight into the interaction dynamics at the molecular level, supporting the superior adsorption properties of NCDs.

Numerous studies (Table 3 ) have established that hydrogels are capable of removing Pb(II) from aqueous solutions although they typically exhibit lower adsorption capacities. The incorporation of nitrogen doping has been identified as an effective approach to enhance these capacities. While considerable research has been directed towards improving the efficiency of adsorption, there has been less focus on the evolution of the active sites of adsorbent and their charge transfer properties during this process. This investigation aims to elucidate the modifications in adsorption mechanisms by monitoring these changes. Existing literature acknowledges the importance of nitrogen and oxygen functional groups in the adsorption of Pb(II). Nonetheless, questions remain regarding the interactions between PbN and PbC binding modes, including whether a competitive relationship exists between these interactions. Moreover, while some research has examined the surface electrical properties of adsorbents, they have generally not accounted for these properties under solution conditions that match the speciation of Pb(II), thereby limiting insights into the specific mechanisms of Pb(II) bonding to amino groups on adsorbents such as HL-NCDs. This work offers a fundamental understanding of how amino groups bond with Pb(II) in solution, making it a crucial reference for the molecular design of carbon dots-derived hydrogel adsorbents.

In summary, the kinetic models indicate that diffusion is the primary mode of movement for Pb(II) on HL-5NCDs, while the Langmuir model demonstrates that monolayer chemical adsorption is the primary mechanism for Pb(II) removal by NCDs. DFT calculations show that the shorter PbN bond leads to stronger adsorption onto NCDs, validating the conclusions from XPS and highlighting the promotional effect of nitrogen doping on adsorption efficiency. Furthermore, the higher binding energy of PbN corroborates the role of amino functional groups as anchor points for Pb. Additionally, combining Bader charge fitting results with Zeta potential analysis examines the charge characteristics of the adsorbent, determining the distribution pattern of charges during adsorption and the effect of charge transitions. Fig. 4d synthesizes these findings, illustrating the comprehensive mechanism of Pb(II) removal from aqueous solutions by HL-5NCDs. The adsorption efficiency of HL-5NCDs is closely associated with its unique structure and chemical properties, facilitating robust interactions with heavy metals and enhancing removal efficiency [68].

This study successfully developed and characterized HL-NCDs, a novel adsorbent created using a straightforward hydrothermal method for efficient removal of Pb(II) from aqueous solutions. The HL-NCDs demonstrated an impressive adsorption capacity of 346.81 mg·g−1 for Pb(II), significantly leveraging the porous structure and rich functional groups of nitrogen and oxygen. The adsorption process is characterized by a combination of chemical reactions and diffusion, which are extensively validated through experimental setups and theoretical analyses focusing on structural and interfacial interactions. These calculations provided more detailed molecular information about their geometry and thermodynamics than adsorption kinetics and thermodynamic analysis alone. The predominant mechanisms for Pb(II) removal involve PbN and PbC, with the strongest interactions occurring between PbN, highlighted by a notable adsorption energy of −0.93 eV and a bond length of 2.44 Å. Furthermore, the HL-NCDs exhibited varying degrees of fluorescence quenching in response to different concentrations of Pb(II), suggesting potential for specific detection applications in water treatment. This research not only provides a green and efficient method for synthesizing high-performance adsorbents but also opens avenues for their application in energy, cleaning, and wastewater pollution remediation, marking a significant advancement in the field of environmental materials science.
